input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. I'll set up a BenchmarkDotNet project targeting both frameworks.
data.reasoningText:
Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project that targets both net9.0 and net10.0 to compare performance of the three methods.

Let me create a benchmark project that:
1. References the TextAnalyzer project
2. Uses BenchmarkDotNet
3. Benchmarks AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount
4. Targets both net9.0 and net10.0

I need to modify the TextAnalyzer project to target both frameworks, or I can create the benchmark project that multi-targets and includes the source file directly.


I think the cleanest approach is to make TextAnalyzer multi-target both frameworks first, then create a benchmark project that also multi-targets and references it. Let me set this up step by step.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-f3f329bfaf514ddcb27529a451cc1911/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-f3f329bfaf514ddcb27529a451cc1911/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-f3f329bfaf514ddcb27529a451cc1911/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <ProjectReference Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.csproj" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.reasoningText:
CoreRuntime.Core100 doesn't exist yet in BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.x. I need to create a custom runtime for .NET 10. Let me check what's available.

I can use `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("net10.0", ".NET 10.0")` or similar. Let me check the BenchmarkDotNet API.

Actually, the standard approach is to use `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion`. Let me try that, or I can use the `[SimpleJob]` attribute approach with the moniker.

Actually, let me just use `Job.Default.WithRuntime(CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("net10.0", ".NET 10.0"))` or similar.
 
I could also pass `--runtimes net9.0 net10.0` via CLI args since BenchmarkDotNet supports that, but net10.0 might not be recognized as a moniker. The safest bet is probably to use `ManualConfig` and create the CoreRuntime manually instead of relying on built-in support.
